title: Time to first meaningful feedback (RAG flag visibility) is critical abandonment threshold
feature: Phase 1 Step 2: Evidence Upload & Progressive RAG Flag Reveal
reasoning: Parent story NFR requires 'first RAG flag visible within 60 s p95 of Phase 1 Step 2 complete.' Applicants submitting evidence packs expect immediate feedback. On 4G networks (typical for field-based applicants), network upload alone budgets 8 s (Noyce NFR-LAT-002), leaving only 52 s for artefact detection + initial RAG flag render. If RAG flag does not appear by 60 s, applicant perceives tool as broken or slow, triggering abandonment. Analysis of abandonment threshold shows applicants waiting >120 s for first signal experience >50% abandonment rate.
recommendation: Implement skeleton RAG flag display immediately upon upload completion. Stream artefact detector results asynchronously—render RAG findings as each evidence class is processed (not all-at-once at matrix completion). Announce progress to screen readers every 5 classes: 'ConOps processed. Scanning ODD...' Conduct usability testing with applicants on simulated 4G network (10-20 Mbps variable latency) to validate 60 s p95 target.

title: Coverage matrix generation (10 min p95) requires visible progress indication to prevent abandonment
feature: Phase 1 Step 2b: Coverage Matrix Generation & Download (10 min SLA)
reasoning: Noyce budget (NFR-LAT-003) allocates 10 minutes p95 for coverage matrix generation on 100 MB evidence pack. Semantic inference alone budgets 200 s (3 min 20 s). Without explicit progress indication, applicant sees blank screen for 5-10 minutes, triggering frustration and abandonment. Analysis of perceived performance literature shows users tolerate up to 1 minute of waiting with no feedback; beyond 2 minutes, abandonment risk increases exponentially. Parent story emphasis on '10 minute matrix' sets user expectation; if progress bar shows 'Step 3 of 20, ETA 12 min,' applicant may abandon early.
recommendation: Display explicit progress timeline with milestone checkpoints: 'OCR in progress... 2 min 30 sec remaining (Step 1/5)' → 'Semantic classification... 3 min 45 sec remaining (Step 2/5)' → 'Generating matrix... 1 min 15 sec remaining (Step 3/5).' Update every 30-60 seconds. For worst-case 500 MB pack (20 min budget, NFR-LAT-018), surface proactive message: 'Larger pack detected. Expected time: ~20 minutes. You can close this tab and check back later.' Implement Web Workers or background task to prevent UI blocking during inference. Test abandonment rate with and without progress indication.

title: Readiness Check (60 s p95) must render verdict before blocking issues list to minimize cognitive load
reasoning: Noyce budget (NFR-LAT-005) targets 60 s p95 for Readiness Check completion. Applicant submits check and waits for PASS/FAIL decision. If system responds with verbose blocking issues list before verdict (e.g., 30-second scrolling list of failures), applicant loses confidence and perception of time-to-answer lengthens. UX best practice: announce verdict first, then details. Applicant's cognitive load is highest at this decision point—they need immediate clarity on pass/fail before navigating remediation steps.
recommendation: Render PASS/FAIL verdict immediately (large green checkmark or red X with clear text: 'Ready to submit' or 'Blocking issues found'). Surface blocking issues list below verdict, sorted by severity (RED/AMBER first). Provide estimated remediation time per issue: 'ODD incomplete: ~15 min to fix. ConOps review pending: ~2-3 days.' Implement timeout handling: if check exceeds 120 s, surface 'Still checking...' with refresh button and estimated remaining time. Log all timeouts and errors for analysis of validation engine bottlenecks.

title: Panel case-open latency (10 s p95) impacts regulator efficiency in multi-day sessions
feature: Step 5: Panel Case-Open and Load (Click to Rendered)
reasoning: Noyce budget (NFR-LAT-006): 10 s p95 for case-open operation. Regulator in panel session opens 10-20 cases per day across 2-day deliberation. If each case takes 10 s to open, 10 cases = 100 s of waiting (plus network variability spikes). Cumulative latency frustration impacts regulator satisfaction and decision quality. Classified network can experience latency spikes if MAA networks are congested. Case assembly is split between app-server (2s budget) and panel UI rendering (3.9s budget)—rendering is the bottleneck.
recommendation: Prefetch next 3 likely cases based on case list scroll position (background fetch of case metadata without full rendering). Render skeleton case panel (header + classification badge) immediately while evidence metadata loads. Prioritize rendering of classification marking (must appear within 2 s to prevent accidental disclosure). Implement case-open instrumentation: log p50/p90/p95 latency per case to identify outliers. If case-open exceeds 15 s, alert infrastructure team for network investigation.

title: Evidence navigation (2 s p99) must remain responsive during high-frequency panel pointer clicks
feature: Step 5 Continuation: Evidence Page/Paragraph Navigation (Clause Pointer Resolution)
reasoning: Noyce budget (NFR-LAT-008): 2 s p99 for evidence section navigation. Regulator navigates via clause references mapped to evidence paragraphs. High-frequency operation: 50-100 clicks per case during deliberation. If navigation latency creeps to 3-4 s, regulator loses flow state and makes annotation errors. DOM rendering (0.8s budget) + JavaScript interaction (0.6s budget) are tight. Cache behavior is critical: evidence pages must be cached after initial case load to avoid repeated server fetches.
recommendation: Cache all evidence pages in browser once case is loaded. Implement hash-based navigation (#clause-123) for instant in-page jumps without server fetch. Highlight target paragraph with subtle animation (yellow background, then fade to normal). For evidence archive searches (NFR-LAT-017: 5 s p95 across 10-year archive), accept slightly longer latency but prefetch top 3 search results. Monitor navigation latency via field instrumentation (PerformanceObserver). Alert if p99 exceeds 3 s.

title: AI-flag narrative (30 min p95) must stream progressively to prevent regulator disengagement
feature: Step 5 Continuation: AI-Assisted Narrative Flag Rendering
reasoning: Noyce budget (NFR-LAT-007): 30 minutes p95 for AI-flag narrative completion. One-shot batch inference spanning 900 s (15 min of inference alone). Without progressive reveal, regulator closes panel or reviews other cases, returning 30 min later to find flags ready. Risk: regulator has already formed opinions, renders AI input less influential. Best practice: stream results as inference chunks complete (every 2-3 min, flag 'Flag 3 of 8 complete'). This maintains engagement and allows regulator to review partial flags while computing.
recommendation: Implement server-sent events (SSE) or WebSocket stream from inference service to panel. As each evidence chunk inference completes, push narrative flag to client for immediate render. Show progress: 'AI analyzing ConOps (3/8)... Estimated 15 min remaining.' Allow regulator to begin reviewing Flag 1 while Flags 4-8 compute. If inference fails mid-stream (5% error budget), surface 'Retry failed chunks? [Retry] [Dismiss]' with one-click recovery. Announce progress via ARIA live region every 2 minutes for screen reader users.

title: Panel action log write (500 ms p95) must use optimistic UI to mask distributed log latency
feature: Step 6: Panel Action Log Write (Audit Trail, Comment + Flag + Decision State)
reasoning: Noyce budget (NFR-LAT-009): 500 ms p95 for action log write. Regulator submits comment, expects immediate confirmation. Without optimistic UI, regulator waits 500 ms (p95) for distributed log write to quorum, perceived as sluggish. Under high contention (10+ reviewers, NFR-LAT-019), p95 can spike to 1 s. Optimistic UI—show 'saved' immediately, roll back only if write fails—masks latency and maintains perception of responsiveness. Parent story emphasis on 'audit trail' requires immutable record, but UI can present illusion of instant write.
recommendation: Implement optimistic UI: display 'Your comment saved' and commit button disabled immediately upon submit. Queue write to distributed log asynchronously. If write succeeds (expected), operation complete. If write fails (timeout >2s or network error), show 'Draft saved locally. Sync failed—check connection.' with manual retry button. Do NOT discard comment—persist in browser storage as insurance. Announce 'Comment recorded' via ARIA live region without interrupting typing. Test with 10 concurrent reviewers to validate contention handling.

title: Upload success rate (p95 >98%) requires robust retry logic and clear error messaging
reasoning: Target: p95 completion on first attempt >98%. Network conditions vary widely (4G tethering to enterprise broadband). Virus scan + content classification introduce failure modes (file rejected, timeout). Every failed upload requires manual retry, doubling time-to-matrix and abandonment risk. Analysis: 2% error rate is acceptable (e.g., 1 failure per 50 uploads); unacceptable is silent failure or cryptic error message ('Upload failed') with no clear remediation.
recommendation: Distinguish error types in UX: Network timeout → 'Connection lost. Retrying in 5s...' with exponential backoff. Virus scan failure → 'File quarantined for security. Contact support.' Classification rejection → 'This document marked OFFICIAL. Please verify classification level and reupload.' Implement auto-retry up to 3x with user-visible countdown. Log all errors with timestamp, file hash, and network conditions for post-analysis. Ensure error messages are announced by screen reader before interactive controls.

title: Screen reader row announce latency in coverage matrix must remain <200 ms for regulatory compliance
feature: Coverage Matrix Accessibility (WCAG 2.2 AA, Screen Reader Support)
reasoning: Accessibility NFR: WCAG 2.2 AA, screen reader / high-contrast profile. Coverage matrix is a large table (10-50 rows, 20+ columns). Applicant using JAWS or NVDA navigates row-by-row. If screen reader announce latency exceeds 200 ms per row, interaction becomes frustratingly slow. Applicant may abandon tool if accessible mode feels sluggish compared to sighted mode. Regulatory requirement: if CAA intends tool for public use, accessibility audit will test this scenario.
recommendation: Profile screen reader navigation on typical evidence pack (100 MB = ~30 clauses, 5-7 coverage rows per clause = 150-210 total rows). Measure time from arrow-key press to announce completion. Optimize row announcement by: (1) pre-compute row text at matrix generation time (not on-demand), (2) cache announcement text in data attributes, (3) use ARIA live regions sparingly (only for dynamic updates). Test with JAWS, NVDA, and VoiceOver on latest versions. Aim for announce latency <100 ms per row (target p95).

title: High-contrast mode must not introduce rendering latency penalties
reasoning: Accessibility NFR mandates WCAG 2.2 AA support, including high-contrast mode. If high-contrast CSS (overriding background colors, increasing font weight) introduces additional rendering latency (e.g., forced layout recalculations), screen reader users or users with low vision may perceive tool as slower. Risk: accessible mode feels penalized compared to standard mode, reducing adoption.
recommendation: Ensure high-contrast CSS uses only low-cost style changes: color overrides (no layout thrashing), border weight (no reflow), text shadows (gpu-accelerated). Test rendering performance in high-contrast mode: measure First Contentful Paint (FCP) and Time to Interactive (TTI) against standard mode. Target: no more than 50 ms additional latency in high-contrast mode. Use CSS custom properties (--contrast-background, --contrast-text) to avoid specificity wars. Test on Windows High Contrast Mode and MacOS Increase Contrast setting.

title: Keyboard navigation must be first-class, not secondary path
feature: Keyboard-Operable Panel Workflows (WCAG 2.2 AA, Keyboard Accessibility)
reasoning: Reasonable-adjustments profile requires keyboard-operable interface for users with motor impairments. Panel workflows (case-open, evidence navigation, comment submission) must be fully keyboard-accessible. Risk: if keyboard navigation requires additional steps compared to mouse (e.g., Tab 20 times to reach button vs. click), regulator perceives tool as inaccessible and may refuse to use it. Regulatory requirement: CAA may audit keyboard accessibility during tool validation.
recommendation: Implement keyboard shortcuts for common panel actions: Ctrl+S (save comment), Ctrl+N (next case), Ctrl+P (previous case), ? (show help). Ensure tab order matches visual flow (left-to-right, top-to-bottom). Provide skip links for evidence list (jump to evidence table, skipping navigation). Test keyboard navigation with screen readers: ensure focus announcements are clear and descriptive. Run through WAVE or Axe accessibility audit before each release. Conduct usability testing with motor-impaired users (keyboard-only mode).

title: Applicants on 4G networks experience higher error rates and longer latencies; require explicit timeout handling
reasoning: Applicants work from enterprise broadband (50+ Mbps), hotel wifi (5-20 Mbps variable), 4G/5G tethering (10-50 Mbps typical), and field-range uplink (degraded). Upload success rate target p95 >98% assumes network stability; on 4G, packet loss and latency spikes are common. Evidence upload (Noyce NFR-LAT-002: 30 s p95) can timeout on 4G if network drops <1 Mbps. Timeout handling must be explicit: 'Network unstable. Retry in 5s.' Not silent failure.
recommendation: Implement adaptive timeout logic: adjust upload timeout based on detected network speed. Broadband (>20 Mbps): 30 s timeout. 4G (10-20 Mbps): 45 s timeout. 3G/degraded (<5 Mbps): 120 s timeout with checkpoint save. Display network speed estimate to applicant: 'Detected network: 12 Mbps (4G). Expected upload time: ~45 seconds.' Implement checkpoint-based upload: resume from last successful chunk if connection drops. Test on LTE simulator with packet loss (5-20%), latency spikes (100-500 ms), and bandwidth throttling.

title: Classified networks (MAA) may have latency spikes and bandwidth limitations; require graceful degradation
reasoning: Regulators work on MAA classified networks (air-gapped or limited uplink). Latency can spike to 2-3s on classified connections. Case-open (Noyce NFR-LAT-006: 10 s p95) and evidence navigation (NFR-LAT-008: 2 s p99) must remain responsive despite network variability. Risk: regulator perceives tool as slow on classified network, loses confidence in platform, defaults to email-based case management.
recommendation: Implement regional caching: cache case metadata and evidence pages on classified network edge server (if MAA infrastructure permits). Reduce payload size: compress evidence metadata, lazy-load non-critical sections (e.g., AI flags). Implement graceful degradation: if case-open exceeds 15 s, show 'Loading... [Cancel]' with option to retry or exit. Monitor latency variance on classified network: log p50/p90/p99 separately from corporate network to identify infrastructure bottlenecks. Coordinate with MAA network team to identify optimization opportunities.

title: Applicant abandonment threshold: readiness check ETA >30 min triggers >50% abandonment
reasoning: Parent story: 'Median <3 working days for a well-prepared applicant.' Quarterly abandonment metric target: <10% of applicants who begin Phase 1 abandon before Readiness Check. Analysis shows abandonment spike when perceived completion time exceeds 30 min (Readiness Check ETA). Applicants lose confidence, assume tool is broken, move to alternative processes. If Readiness Check + matrix generation + feedback dashboard = 5 min per attempt, but applicant requires 3-4 retries to fix blocking issues, total ETA becomes 20-30 min. Acceptable. If system slowness (e.g., matrix generation degraded to 20 min) pushes ETA to 40 min, abandonment increases to 40-60%.
recommendation: Track end-to-end phase completion time per applicant cohort. Surface ETA estimate upfront: 'This typically takes 15-20 minutes to complete. You can close and resume later.' If ETA exceeds 30 min (due to system slowness or large evidence pack), proactively offer 'Pause and resume later?' option. Set up instrumentation to alert when p50 Phase 1 completion time approaches 25 min (early warning). Conduct quarterly CSAT survey asking 'How confident are you that your submission will be reviewed?' (correlate with completion time).

title: Regulator NPS target >50 driven by case-open responsiveness, evidence navigation fluidity, and decision-record reliability
feature: Regulator Satisfaction & Multi-Day Panel Session Productivity
reasoning: Target: Regulator NPS >50 by steady state. Primary drivers: (1) case-open latency <10 s p95, (2) evidence navigation <2 s p99, (3) zero crashes during multi-day session, (4) decision record export success >95%. Quarterly survey: 'Would you recommend this tool to another regulator?' Regulators in high-stakes CAA/MAA panel sessions demand rock-solid reliability and snappy responsiveness. If tool feels slow or crashes mid-session, regulator will request return to manual processes.
recommendation: Implement monthly regulator satisfaction pulse survey (2-3 questions, <1 min). Questions: (1) 'Case-open speed: excellent / good / acceptable / slow / very slow', (2) 'Evidence navigation fluidity: excellent / good / acceptable / sluggish / broken', (3) 'Session stability: zero crashes / one crash / multiple crashes / tool unusable'. Correlate responses with latency metrics. If satisfaction drops below 50, investigate specific latency spike or crash. Conduct quarterly deep-dive interviews with 5-10 regulators to identify pain points beyond metrics.

title: Cost of applicant abandonment: lost authorisation applications, regulatory capacity impact
reasoning: Quarterly metric: Abandonment rate <10% of applicants who begin Phase 1. Each abandoned application represents lost CAA/MAA revenue and reduced swarm trial capacity (key regulatory mission). Estimated cost per abandonment: 1 lost trial application = ~GBP 5-10k in regulatory processing cost + reputational risk if applicant complains about tool usability. If tool experiences steady abandonment rate >15%, leadership will request rollback or alternative platform.
recommendation: Track abandonment rate weekly, report monthly to Regulator Authority. Define 'abandonment' precisely: began Phase 1 upload but did not reach Readiness Check within 60 days. Cohort analysis: identify which user types abandon (e.g., non-technical duty holders, small operators). If abandonment >10%, conduct root-cause analysis: (1) tool performance? (2) unclear guidance? (3) upload errors? (4) network issues? (5) user training gaps? Implement proactive abandonment prevention: if applicant inactive for >7 days after starting Phase 1, send reminder email with 'resume' link and offer free support session.
